coredata.dc:description: Water pollution poses an increasing global threat to the environment and sustainable development of human society. The development of novel materials and technologies hold the promise for highly efficient detection and treatment of water contaminants from various industries that playing an essential role in both social and economic benefits. Recently, carbon dots (CDs) emerge as a novel type of low-dimensional carbon nanomaterials with the advantages of high photo/thermal stability, negligible photobleaching, excellent biocompatibility and low environmental toxicity, holding immense potential for toxic-element determination and pollution control in aquatic environments. In this review, we aim to summarize the recent progress in the design and synthesis of CDs-based nanomaterials and nanocomposites, and highlight their applications in the detection and treatment of water contaminants. Furthermore, the potential and problems of CDs-based nanocomposites are discussed with the aim to achieve fruitful results in the high-sensitive detection and high-efficiency removal of water contaminants from different sources.

head.ce:title: Recent advances of carbon dots for the detection and removal of water contaminants

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Water pollution poses an increasing global threat to the environment and sustainable development of human society. The development of novel materials and technologies hold the promise for highly efficient detection and treatment of water contaminants from various industries that playing an essential role in both social and economic benefits. Recently, carbon dots (CDs) emerge as a novel type of low-dimensional carbon nanomaterials with the advantages of high photo/thermal stability, negligible photobleaching, excellent biocompatibility and low environmental toxicity, holding immense potential for toxic-element determination and pollution control in aquatic environments. In this review, we aim to summarize the recent progress in the design and synthesis of CDs-based nanomaterials and nanocomposites, and highlight their applications in the detection and treatment of water contaminants. Furthermore, the potential and problems of CDs-based nanocomposites are discussed with the aim to achieve fruitful results in the high-sensitive detection and high-efficiency removal of water contaminants from different sources.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: The rapid development of science and technology brings mankind plenty of profits as well as severe environmental deterioration. A large amount of raw chemicals and untreated drugs wastewater have been discharged into the natural water system, leading to serious water pollution [1,2]. The current methods for the wastewater treatment can be classified as flocculation, coagulation, adsorption, precipitation, filtration, activated sludge process and membrane technology, etc. [3–5]. Currently, various contaminants, (e.g., antibiotic drugs, pharmaceuticals, pesticides and organic dyes), are practically difficult to remove through traditional water treatment technologies. The disposal of untreated wastewater into surface water will not only cause significant damage to the aquatic ecosystem but also harmful to human health after a long-term accumulation. Therefore, it is of great importance to meet the increased demand for detecting and treating pollutants for wastewater from different sources. Although there are several detection techniques for high-sensitivity water contaminants, these technologies generally have certain disadvantages, such as large-scale instruments and difficulty in achieving rapid and on-field detection. Toward the improvement of the aquatic environment, it is urgent to develop novel approaches to identify and remove emerging contaminants in complex environmental media with high sensitivity and good selectivity. For instance, adsorption has long been used as a practical means to remove contaminants from wastewater due to the balance between the removal performance and cost-effectiveness. However, traditional sorbent materials have an unsolved issue in material regeneration and high production cost, which considerably limit their broad application. Advanced oxidation processes (AOPs) exhibit outstanding removal capabilities for refractory and complex organic pollutants. AOPs can effectively degrade organic pollutants into less hazardous or harmless substances, or even convert them into water and carbon dioxide through highly oxidizing hydroxyl radicals.

The emergence of low-dimensional nanomaterials brings new opportunities in many fields, including energy conversion, photocatalysis, bioimaging, biosensors, drug delivery and electrocatalysis. In particular, low-dimensional carbon nanomaterials, such as fullerenes, carbon nanotubes (CNTs), graphene oxides (GOs) and carbon dots (CDs), have attracted considerable attention in the environmental field [6]. Emerging as a kind of newly developed carbon nanomaterials, CDs with a typical particle size below 20 nm that was first discovered and isolated by Xu et al., in 2004 during the post-treatment of as-synthesized single-walled CNTs [7]. In 2006, Sun et al. reported the synthesis of fluorescent carbon nanoparticles by laser ablation, and coined them the term ‘carbon dots’ [8]. Typically, CDs are composed of sp2-conjugated carbon cores, surface and/or edge functional groups, which could be divided into carbon nanodots, carbon polymer dots, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with respect to the synthetic methods and underlying luminescence mechanisms [9]. Due to the negligible fluorescence photobleaching, low environmental toxicity, good water solubility, CDs has broad application prospects in the selective and sensitive detection of toxic-metal ions, adsorption and degradation of various contaminants from industrial wastewater [10].

The fluorescence property of CDs is one of the most fascinating features for the detection of specific species [11]. Taking the advantage of abundant surface and edge functional groups, CDs are highly susceptible to interaction with water contaminants, resulting in the significant changes in their emission spectra. Therefore, fluorescent probes based on CDs were designed and applied in the determination of various water contaminants, exhibiting the unique advantages of quick detection, high sensitivity, in-situ testing, low production cost and low environment toxicity. A large number of functional groups on the CDs surface (e.g., amino, hydroxyl and carboxyl groups) will significantly contribute to the adsorption of pollutants. Moreover, tailorable optical properties and charge transfer properties of CDs lay the foundation for their further development in the photodegradation of water contaminants. Due to the recent achievement of CDs in the selective and sensitive detection, adsorption, and degradation of pollutants in the aqueous environment, CDs and their nanocomposites in application of water pollution treatment have attracted more attentions. Here, we summarize recent progress and discuss prospects and challenges for further development of CDs in the selective and sensitive detection, removal and degradation of contaminants in the water environment.
1
Synthesis of CDs

The synthetic strategy of CDs could be divided into top-down and bottom-up routes [12] (Fig. 1 ). On the one hand, the top-down route mainly involves exfoliating carbon sources (e.g., carbon nanotubes, graphite powder and carbon black) with large size into smaller-size nanoparticles by arc discharge [7], laser ablation [8], acid oxidation [13], and electrochemical methods [14] that is suitable for the industrial production of CDs through a wide range of low-cost raw materials. On the other hand, the bottom-up route has been widely adopted to obtain CDs by pyrolysis or carbonization of small molecular precursors through a hydro/solvothermal [15–18] treatment or microwave-assisted process [19]. In the recent years, CDs that synthesized from the bottom-up methods have received increasing attentions due to their size-dependent multicolor emission and high photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) in comparison with products through the top-down methods.

Recently, a variety of sustainable biomass materials were employed to synthesize CDs, holding the promise for the low-cost and large-scale synthesis of CDs. According to previous reports, CDs have been prepared by various types of biomasses, such as banana [20], sweet potato [21], peanut shells [22], honey [23], milk [24], orange juice [25], lemon juice [26], coffee beans [27], coffee grounds [28], starch [29], meat [30] and eggs [31]. The PLQYs of biomass-based CDs are generally low and their formation mechanism remain unclear, but the exploration of biomass-derived CDs through inexpensive and easily-accessible raw materials and green synthesis methods will open up new opportunities for practical applications.

More importantly, exploring novel synthetic approaches for the rapid mass production of CDs could significantly support for the further advancement of the detection and treatment of wastewater contaminants. Some newly-developed strategies have been employed to achieve a large-scale preparation of CDs from gram-scale to kilogram-scale, including hydro/solvothermal, microwave-assisted heating, magnetic hyperthermia, self-exothermic, solvent-free routines and so on [32–40]. Although the realization of mass production of CDs paves a promising avenue toward broadening their potential applications, the rational design and synthesis of CDs with high PLQYs for full-color emission remains a considerable challenge. Recently, the radical-assisted synthetic approach has been exploited for the large-scale preparation of CDs with remarkable PLQY (>80 %) [41,42]. Featuring as high-yield production, short-time reaction and room-temperature operation, the radical-assisted synthetic method could be considered one of the most promising strategies for the fundamental research and practical commercialization of CDs and CDs-based nanocomposites.
2
CDs in the detection of environment contaminants in water

The use of artificial chemicals plays an indispensable role in improving the quality of human life but a heavy burden on the water quality under an untreated discharge scenario. The complex and diverse pollutants are in fact difficult to remove, which seriously threatens the public health. The sensitive, rapid, and specific detection of pollutants in water bodies is crucial for accessing water quality and tracing the pollutant sources. The currently used approaches for the determination of pollutant mainly rely on UV–Vis absorption spectroscopy, fluorescence spectroscopy [43,44], electrochemical methods [45], high-performance liquid chromatography [46], and atomic absorption spectroscopy [47]. However, these traditional analytical methods have the obvious downsides for the rapid and on-field detection, such as expensive instruments, complicated and time-consuming sample pretreatment process. Fluorescence detection technology has been attracting particular attention owing to the exceptional characteristics of rapid response, high detection sensitivity and good selectivity for heavy-metal ions.

Emerging as a novel type of fluorescent carbon nanomaterials, the luminescence properties of CDs can be tailored by modulating particle size, surface functionalization [48], surface passivation [48], heteroatom doping [49,50]. Moreover, CDs exhibit good chemical stability, photostability and water solubility, providing an ideal platform for the design and application of high-performance fluorescent probes. In fact, the design of CDs-based fluorescent probes is based on the interaction between CDs and analytes, which leads to either the fluorescence quenching or enhancement as well as emission wavelengths shift. Currently, a series of CDs-based fluorescent probes have been explored for the detection of heavy-metal ions, pesticides, antibiotics and other contaminants.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of CDs-based fluorescent probes for the detection of water contaminants

ce:section.ce:para: Photo-induced electron transfer (PET) features as electron transfer between electron donor and electron acceptor under photoexcitation, resulting in the fluorescence quenching [51–53]. Depending on the electron transfer pathway (Fig. 2 a), PET can be generally classified into a-PET and d-PET process [54,55], respectively. Upon photoexcitation, the electron transfer occurs from the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) of the fluorophore to its lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO). Once the HOMO energy level of the free receptor is higher than the corresponding fluorophore, electrons from the HOMO of the receptor can be energetically transferred to the HOMO of the fluorophore through a-PET process, leading to the fluorescence quenching since the photoexcited electron will be unable to return to its HOMO. Alternatively, in d-PET process, photoexcited electrons on the LUMO of fluorophore are transferred to the LUMO of the receptor, quenching the fluorescence of fluorophore. Upon binding to the analyte, the d-PET process is then blocked, thereby causing the fluorescence recovery.

Intramolecular charge-transfer (ICT) based fluorescent probes have a typical D-π-A structure that the electron donor (D) and electron acceptor (A) functional blocks are attached to each side of the fluorophore, featuring D-π-A push–pull system in the excited state (Fig. 2b). Either the electron-donating or electron-accepting part could act as a receptor. The charge transfer from the electron-donating unit to the electron-accepting part is determined within the molecule upon photoexcitation. When an analyte combines with the receptor, the electron-pushing abilities and electron-pulling abilities of the ICT based fluorescent probe can be influenced due to the change of charge distribution in the whole probe molecule, leading to either hypochromic or bathochromic shift in the fluorescence spectra [56–58]. As an analyte binds to the electron-donating part, the degree of molecular conjugation is reduced, therefore leads to the hypochromic emission. As the target analyte interacts with the electron-accepting unit of the ICT probe, bathochromic shift could be observed in the emission spectra owing to the increase of the conjugation degree.

Fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) is a non-radiative energy transfer from an excited-state donor fluorophore to the adjacent ground-state acceptor fluorophore, featuring as distance-dependent process. In this case, the donor fluorescence is reduced or even quenched while the acceptor fluorescence can be either enhanced or quenched (Fig. 2c). For the FRET process, two requirements are required as follow, one is that the spatial distance between the donor and acceptor that is in the range of 1–10 nm; and the other one is that a significant overlap between the absorption spectrum of the acceptor and the emission spectrum of the donor [59–61].

The fluorescence internal-filter effect (IFE) mostly refers to the emission of the fluorophores is reduced or even completely quenched due to the absorption by the other fluorophores (Fig. 2d). Toward improving the detection sensitivity, the high-performance IFE based fluorescent probes can be designed and further prepared through modulating spectral overlap between the excitation/emission spectra of the emitter and absorption spectra of the light-absorbing substances [62–65].

Some CDs exhibit bright fluorescence in dilute solution but weak or even quenched fluorescence in the aggregated or solid state. Previous studies reveal that the aggregation-induced quenching (ACQ) effect has limited the practical process in the utilizations of CDs. The aggregation-induced emission (AIE) was first highlighted by Tang et al., in 2001 [66]. In contrast to ACQ effect, AIE fluorophores characteristically exhibits no fluorescence in a dilute solution but bright fluorescence in the solid state. Taking advantage of this unique enhancement phenomenon, the design of CDs-based fluorescent probes based on the AIE effect has now been employed for the selective detection of tetracycline antibiotics [67].

ce:section.ce:section-title: CDs as fluorescent probes for heavy-metal ions detection
ce:section.ce:para: The development of industry relies heavily on the use of various metal-containing compounds, leading to metal ions as one of the most common types of pollutants in wastewater. Excessive metal ions are found to be detrimental to humans and the ecosystem, especially heavy-metal ions, (e.g., Cd2+, Pb2+, Cu2+ and Ag+) [68–70]. Some of heavy-metal ions are so toxic that even low concentrations could cause serious tissue damage to humans, such as Cr(VI), As(V), Hg(II) [71,72]. It is necessary to achieve fast and accurate detection for these toxic-metal ions. Thanks to the existence of surface functional groups, the luminescence properties of CDs could be influenced by the presence of metal ions in the water bodies, thus achieving the selective detection of heavy-metal ions.

It is well-known that chromium (Cr) is one of the most widely used elements in the leather tanning as well as electroplating industries. First, Cr(VI) have strong biological toxicity which can be easily absorbed by the human body. Even a small amount of Cr(VI) could cause serious skin allergy or even lead to cancerization in some serious cases. Second, Cr(VI) ions is extremely harmful to the environment, and excessive Cr(VI) ions is lethal to aquatic organisms, it is of great significance to achieve accurate monitoring of Cr(VI) in water bodies. Cai et al. [73] prepared N-doped GQDs by hydrothermal reaction of xylan with the assistance of NaOH/urea for detecting Cr(VI). The N-doped GQDs demonstrated an excellent selectivity for Cr(VI) ions (Fig. 3 a) while the presence of other ions can hardly affected the Cr(VI) detection (Fig. 3b). The passivation layer of N-doped GQDs could be destroyed by the strong oxidants (e.g., K2Cr2O7) due to the incomplete surface passivation through the carbonization of xylan. A selective fluorescence quenching in the presence of Cr(VI) was unveiled while no significant interference was found in the presence of other metal ions or anions. The as-prepared N-doped GQDs exhibit a high sensitivity for Cr(VI) detection (Fig. 3c), and the fluorescence intensity of N-doped GQDs decreased with the increase of Cr(VI) concentration. The limit of detection (LOD) of N-doped GQDs for Cr(VI) was found to be 22 ppm according to the relationship between Cr(VI) ion concentration and corresponding fluorescence intensity. More importantly, a portable detection platform was successfully designed through the combination of microfluidic chip and smartphone, offering a convenient approach for the rapid in-situ detection of Cr(VI).

Dual/multi-emission fluorescent probes can further improve the accuracy and reliability for the detection and avoid the influences caused by external environmental factors [75–77]. Naveen et al. [74] reported the facile preparation of water-soluble double-emission nitrogen-doped CDs (DECDs) by a microwave-assisted technique. DECDs showed a good photostability under continuous UV excitation as well as in a wide range of pH values and temperature. The DECDs exhibited good selectivity to Cr(VI) and Fe(III) and a good linear correlation between the concentrations of Cr(VI) and Fe(III) and fluorescence intensities (Fig. 3d–f). The photographs taken at the daylight and UV lamp lines are shown in Fig. 3g and h. It should be noted that the highly toxic Cr(VI) could be converted to the form of Cr(III) with much less toxicity, extending the application of DECDs.

Currently, silver ion (Ag+) is extensively employed in water purification processes owing to its strong antibacterial properties. Nevertheless, as one kind of heavy-metal ions, Ag+ could cause a serious impact on aquatic organisms and human body. The “on-off” type CDs fluorescent probe was prepared on the basis of the selective fluorescence quenching of CDs by the addition of Ag+ [78–80]. The “on-off” fluorescent probes are susceptible to other quenchers in the environment, but the “on-off” fluorescent probes can effectively eliminate the effect of environmental interference. Huang et al. [81] reported the synthesis of nitrogen, boron and sulfur co-doped CDs (NBS-CDs) by a hydrothermal treatment of 2,5-diaminobenzenesulfonic acid and 3-aminophenylboronic acid, which was further employed as an “on-off-on” probe for Ag+ and L-cysteine (L-Cys) detection. Since Ag+ exhibited a stronger binding ability to L-Cys than NBS-CDs, the fluorescence intensity of NBS-CDs was found to be gradually recovered after the addition of L-Cys. The good linear relationship between Ag+ concentration and fluorescence intensity of NBS-CDs solution was revealed as Ag+ concentration increasing from 1 to 300 μM. Moreover, the LOD of NBS-CDs for Ag+ and L-Cys detection was found to be 0.35 and 0.045 μM, exhibiting a highly resistant to external interferences.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CDs as fluorescent probes for antibiotic detection
ce:section.ce:para: Antibiotics are considered one of the most significant inventions of the 20th century. Antibiotics are not only used for disease treatment, but also widely used in all aspects of human daily life, such as agricultural production and livestock production. Although the widespread use of antibiotics benefits humans, but the antibiotics can not be completely absorbed by the animal or human body due to the metabolism. Nevertheless, traditional water treatment process of urban sewage treatment plant is less effective for removing antibiotics, thus leading to the accumulation of antibiotics to the ecosystems as well as potential threats to human health. In fact, antibiotics and their degradation intermediates have detrimental effects on aquatic organisms and even cause harm to humans through the food chain. Moreover, antibiotics discharged into natural water bodies can easily lead to antibiotic resistance in aquatic organisms, and generation and spread of antibiotic resistance genes [82–85]. Therefore, the establishment of sensitive, accurate and rapid antibiotic detection approaches are of great importance to monitor antibiotic pollution and reduce antibiotic pollution.

Tetracycline (TC) is considered one of the most important and widely used antibiotic categories, causing serious harm to the aquatic environment due to the difficulty in degradation [86]. Yuan [87] synthesized CDs by using o-phenylenediamine and glyoxal as carbon sources toward the selective and sensitive detection of TC. Fig. 4 a, b shows a quantitative relationship between the concentration of TC and change in fluorescence intensity of CDs, exhibiting LOD value of 6 μM. Subsequently, a combined fluorescent probe method and high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) was performed to determine the TC content in a 0.25 g tablet while the detection results were found to be 0.2480 g and 0.2445 g, respectively, which is well consistent with the tablet specification, demonstrating the clinical potential in the rapid and accurate detection of TC.

The wastewater from different sources, such as pharmaceutical industry, hospital and domestic wastewater, often contains multiple antibiotics. Currently, the selective detection of antibiotic is becoming a hot issue. Using lysine as the precursor, Yu et al. [88] reported the synthesis of CDs toward the detection of seven antibiotics, achieving accurate identification by the change of CDs fluorescence due to the complexation of Al3+ with different antibiotics (Fig. 4c).

Molecular blotting techniques were successfully employed to specifically identify target molecules by molecular imprinting polymers (MIPs) for the detection and removal of contaminants in complex environments [90,91]. The synthesis of different types of MIPs-coated CDs composites took the advantage of the high sensitivity from fluorescent CDs and high selectivity from molecular blotting techniques. Using sweet potato peels as bioprecursor, Liu et al. [89] designed a novel type of fluorescent probe, namely MIP-coated CDs, by a sol-gel method for oxytetracycline (OTC) detection (Fig. 4d). The fluorescent signal of MIP-coated CDs was quenched after adding OTC into the testing system. The LOD was found to be as low as 15.3 ng/mL, and the detection mechanism was shown in Fig. 4e. To investigate the selective detection ability of MIP-coated CDs, chlortetracycline (CTC), TC and OTC was investigated due to their similar molecular structures, demonstrating an imprinting factor (IF) of 2.5, 2.3 and 4.8, respectively. Although both CTC and TC exhibited fluorescence quenching by the use of MIP-coated CDs, the strongest affinity for the template molecule OTC allowed for selective detection of OTC.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CDs as fluorescent probes for pesticides detection
ce:section.ce:para: The pesticide production wastewater features as high toxicity, complex composition and poor biochemical properties, leading to the serious contamination in soil-plant-animal-human food chain [92,93]. Therefore, it is urgent to effectively monitor and detect pesticide pollution in water bodies, relying on the newly-developed materials and methods for qualitative and quantitative analysis. Previous results revealed that the preparation of heteroatom-doped CDs could be considered a platform to modulate their optical properties [94–96]. Li et al. [97] synthesized a serious of CDs, including N-CD-Fla, NS-CD-Fla and NSB-CD-Fla, by the facile hydrothermal method with different doping elements using Ginkgo biloba flavonoids extract as carbon source. N-CD-Fla exhibits yellowish-green emission while NS-CD-Fla, and NSB-CD-Fla shows the blue and greenish-blue luminescence, respectively. It should be noticed that chemical doping of nitrogen and nitrogen/sulfur significantly enhances the fluorescence of CDs, while the introduction of B has unexpectedly adverse effect on PLQY. NS-CD-Fla exhibits a higher PLQY due to a higher nitrogen content and nitrogen-sulfur synergistic enhancement. N-CD-Fla, NS-CD-Fla and NSB-CD-Fla could be used for the selective detection of fenitrothion, dithion, dinoseb and other analogues with the LOD of 0.36, 0.28, and 0.66 nM, respectively.

The CDs-based fluorescence-enhanced probes have lower detection limits and higher sensitivity than fluorescence-quenched probes, exhibiting better results in the detection of trace contaminants. Mohapatra et al. [98] synthesized N-doped CDs (o-PCD) by a modified hydrothermal treatment and used for herbicide atrazine detection. Upon addition of atrazine, the -NH of atrazine formed hydrogen bonds with NH3 + of o-PCD, which led to a gradual increase in fluorescence intensity through AIE. And the LOD was as low as 3 pM at pH = 5.

Considering an upward trend in living standards, the demand for clean drinking water is steadily increasing. The current water treatment methods can be divided into three mainstream categories: physical, chemical and biological methods. The choice of treatment process mainly depends on the features of the wastewater, such as composition, contamination concentration, pH value, cost, etc. Among many water treatment methods, adsorption has been practically used due to the advantage in simplicity, inexpensive cost, facile operation, environmental friendliness and without generation of secondary pollution [99,100]. The adsorption efficiency is closely related to the type of adsorbent [101,102]. The commonly used adsorbents include activated carbon [103], clay minerals [104], and agricultural wastes [105], but their application in practice is still limited by reusability and adsorption performance. Recently, CDs have been explored as promising adsorbents due to the abundant functional groups on their surface and edge sites that could considerably increase the adsorption capacity [106,107].

Adsorption have been regarded a mass transfer process in which contaminants in the water bodies are immobilized on the surface of CDs-based adsorbent materials for removing the contaminants, which could be divided into physical adsorption and chemical adsorption, respectively. Physical adsorption is the adsorption process of pollutants onto the adsorbent materials by van der Waals interaction while chemical adsorption is the adsorption of pollutants onto the adsorbent material by forming ionic or covalent bonds. Furthermore, the adsorption mechanisms for different pollutants by CDs-based adsorbents could be distinguished by hydrophobic interaction, electrostatic force, π-conjugated interaction, hydrogen bonding, and surface complexation. Researches toward the practical application, the binding of pollutants to CDs rely on the above-mentioned multiple mechanisms, therefore achieving the efficient adsorption.

Hydrophobic interaction is reflected as the spontaneous aggregation between nonpolar molecules or nonpolar groups in aqueous solutions relying on charge affinity [108]. Previous reports exhibited that hydrophobic interaction is one of the well-investigated mechanisms for the adsorption of organic pollutants by carbon-based materials [109–111]. Li et al. [112] synthesized nanocomposite on the basis of CDs, poly (glycidyl methacrylate) (PGMA) and Fe3O4 NPs, which is denoted hereafter as CDs@PGMA@Fe3O4 NPs, for magnetic solid phase extraction (MSPE) of polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs) in water environment. CDs played an indispensable role in the MSPE procedure after linking Fe3O4 NPs through PGMA chains. PAHs could be efficiently adsorbed on the surface of CDs through π-conjugation interactions and hydrophobic interactions. The PGMA chains acted as the connecting chain between Fe3O4 NPs to CDs which not only regulated the loading number of CDs and enhanced the hydrophobicity between adsorbent composite and PAHs compounds. It should be noted that the increase of polymer chains could considerably enhance the hydrophobicity, thus realizing the efficient adsorption of PAHs.

Electrostatic interactions are considered another effective approach for the adsorption of pollutants by CDs. Recently, Yuan et al. [113]. Report the preparation of a novel lignin-based hydrogel formed by CDs and cellulose nanofibers. Thanks to a large amount of amino groups on the surface of CDs, Cr(VI) could be efficiently adsorbed on the hydrogel surface in a short time via the electrostatic interaction. The ion transport channels were constructed by 3D pores inside of the hydrogel, Cr(VI) ions was transported from the hydrogel surface to its interior, thus exhibiting the good adsorption capacity.

The adsorption capacities of adsorbents rely on electrostatic interactions that are usually affected by the solution pH value. Since CDs possess rich surface functional groups, the change in solution pH value could drive either protonation or deprotonation. El-Shamy et al. [114] reported the embedding of CDs modified zinc peroxide (ZnO2) into polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) films (PVA/CZnO2) for the adsorption of methylene blue (MB). Organic dyes could be removed through the electrostatic interaction (Fig. 5 a), hydrogen-bonding interaction and π-conjugated interaction between PVA/CZnO2 adsorbent and MB. The functional groups on the PVA/CZnO2 surface were protonated in acidic medium and the large amount of H+ of electrolyte intensified the competition with MB on the surface of catalyst, leading to a significant decrease in the adsorption efficiency. However, the deprotonation of surface groups on the PVA surface results in the increase of negatively-charged surface groups on the adsorbent, thus increasing the adsorption of MB in alkaline medium. (Fig. 5a).

The mechanism of pollutants adsorption by CDs-based adsorbents can be attributed to hydrogen bonding as well. Considering the existence of various surface/edge functional groups (e.g., -NH2 -OH, and -COOH) on CDs, the formation of hydrogen bonds could significantly enhance the adsorption performance. Zhang et al. [115] designed the nanocomposites by introducing CDs into layered double hydroxide (LDH) for the adsorption and removal of the cationic dye. The addition of CDs enriched hydroxyl groups on the LDH surface that led to the formation hydrogen bonds with the N- and O-containing surface groups of MB. Owing to the synergistic effect of hydrogen bond and electrostatic interaction, CDs/LDH delivered a good adsorption performance (Fig. 5 b).

Typically, π-conjugation interaction is relatively weak interaction between aromatic rings, electron-rich and electron-deficient species, which could be considered one of effective strategies for developing sustainable adsorbents. In principle, organic pollutants could be bonded to carbon-based adsorbents to achieve the adsorption and removal of pollutants [116,117]. CDs modified magnetic CNTs adsorbents were prepared by Deng et al. [118]. The existence of π-conjugation interactions among CDs, CNTs and carbamazepine was confirmed by Raman characterization. The aromatic and heterocyclic rings of carbamazepine could interact with CDs and CNTs, which exhibited effective removal of carbamazepine due to the existence of π-electron-rich unit and π-electron-depleted region.

The adsorption of heavy-metal ions by CDs-based adsorbents could be more commonly attributed to surface complexation [119]. Through surface complexation, complexes were formed by the binding between the surface groups on the CDs and central heavy-metal ions [120]. The surface groups of CDs and corresponding nanocomposites tend to provide more adsorption sites. Huang et al. [121] prepared polyethyleneimine functionalized CQDs (PECQDs) and then introduce MnFe2O4 for the construction of a bifunctional nanocomposite PECQDs/MnFe2O4, demonstrating a good adsorption performance due to the presence of complexes from -OH, -NH2 surface groups.

Dye wastewaters coming from a wide range of sources feature as high chromaticity and organic content have been considered one of the most important tasks for wastewater treatment [122,123]. Adsorption method has been considered the first-choice for dye wastewater decolorization with respect to good adsorption performance and acceptable cost. Wang et al. [124] prepared nanocomposite by grafting CDs onto polyacrylonitrile fibers (PANF), which is hereafter denoted as PANF-g-CDs (Fig. 6 a). PANF-g-CD could effectively adsorb anionic organic dyes from dye mixture solution (Fig. 6b–d). It should be highlighted that PANF-g-CD maintained good adsorption performance even after 10 cycles. Taking the advantage of the fluorescent characteristic of PANF-g-CD, the in-situ detection of the dye adsorption-desorption process could be conducted (Fig. 6e). Suman and co-workers synthesized CDs through a hydrothermal treatment of lemon juice and cysteine and further prepared hydrogels by cross-linking carboxylation with polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP) [125]. Compared to individual PVP, PVP-CDs with an enlarged specific surface area is found to be more favorable for contaminants adsorption. Moreover, the adsorption capacity of individual CDs in solution was systematically investigated by using the cationic dyes malachite green (MG), crystalline violet (CV), and anionic dye eosin Y (EY) as model contaminants (Fig. 6f), and the equilibrium adsorption capacities of MG, CV, and EY on the CDs surface reached 5.75, 5.3, and 8.75 mg/g, respectively.

Due to a wide range of pollution sources, heavy-metal contaminants are found to be difficult to metabolize and non-degradable. In particular, heavy-metal ions could lead to the serious and irreversible damage to animal and human body due to food chain and drinking water accumulation. Therefore, the efficient removal of heavy-metal ions from water bodies is of paramount importance. According to recent reports, the adsorption of heavy-metal ions could be accomplished through electrostatic interactions, surface complexation and other mechanisms for CDs. Luo et al. [126] reported the preparation of amino-modified CDs and further fabricated onto the surface of carboxymethylated cellulose nanofiber by condensation reaction to form CM-CNF with grafted CDs (CM-CNF-CDs), and finally a fluorescent hydrogel with 3D porous structure was synthesized by radical polymerization (Fig. 7 a and b) for enhancing the adsorption capabilities toward metal ions. FT-IR characterization verified the successful grafting of CDs to CM-CNF (Fig. 7c). The maximum adsorption capacities for Cr6+, Ba2+, Pb2+, and Cu2+ could reach 534.5, 271.9, 789.6, and 98.2 mg/g, respectively (Fig. 7d). The adsorption isotherms of Cr6+ were studied, indicating the adsorption process was consistent with the Langmuir model (Fig. 7e). It can be concluded that the amide, hydroxyl and carboxyl groups on the fluorescent hydrogel play a key role in adsorbing Cr6+ (Fig. 7f).

Removing antibiotics by adsorption method can efficiently reduce the negative impact of antibiotics and their metabolites in water bodies [127,128]. The adsorption procedure of CDs-based adsorbents for antibiotic adsorption generally involves electrostatic interactions, π-π interactions, hydrophobic effects, ion exchange, etc. Liu et al. [129] synthesized NiFe2O4-carbon dot (NiFeC) nanocomposites using a one-pot solvothermal method. The considerably increased adsorption capacity of NiFeC for TC could be ascribed to the large number of -OH and -COOH on the surface of CDs. NiFeC composite exhibited a higher TC adsorption performance in comparison with traditional adsorbents, such as activated carbon, bentonite clay, zeolite molecular sieves, macroreticular resin, and diatomite. A maximum adsorption capacity of 591.72 mg/g and removal efficiency of NiFeC composite for TC remained as high as 90 % after five adsorption-desorption cycles. Currently, the high concentration of antibiotics after adsorption could be further degraded by other approaches, such as aerobic and anaerobic biological treatment technologies, to achieve the purification of wastewater. In particular, some carbon-based materials could not only adsorb but also degrade antibiotics by AOPs, such as photocatalysis, ozonation and Fenton oxidation [130].

ce:section.ce:section-title: CDs-based composite in AOPs for contaminants removal
ce:section.ce:para: AOPs was previously proposed by Glaze et al. [131] in the 1980s to purify water by generating hydroxyl radicals (OH·) with strong oxidizing properties to completely mineralize pollutants, exhibiting promising performance for the treatment of refractory organic pollutants and removal of certain inorganic pollutants [132,133]. AOPs technologies included photocatalytic oxidation, Fenton oxidation, and ozone oxidation process that are already commonly used in water pollution control.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CDs-based material in photocatalytic degradation of water contaminants
ce:section.ce:para: Photocatalysis oxidation, as a kind of AOPs, features as facile operation and high degradation efficiency. Currently, CDs have been developed as promising candidates for the design of new photocatalysts system. Although there are still some problems in the application of CDs-based photocatalysts, the addition of CDs could significantly improve the photocatalytic performance compared with traditional photocatalysts, such as TiO2 and g-C3N4 [134–138]. Aiming at overcoming the problems of low solar energy utilization efficiency and avoiding the recombination of photogenerated charge carrier of traditional photocatalysts, CDs exhibit great potential in photocatalytic degradation of environmental pollutants in water bodies.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic degradation mechanisms of CDs and CDs-based materials
ce:section.ce:para: CDs have recently explored as both photocatalysts and co-catalysts in the photocatalytic treatment of contaminants, and the mechanisms could be mainly attributed to the following: (1) promoting the generation and separation of photogenerated hole/electron pairs; (2) suppressing the recombination of hole/electron pairs; (3) broadening the response range of photocatalysts, which all contribute to boost the removal efficiency.

CDs have recently been explored as photocatalysts due to the formation of hole/electron pair with strong redox ability, which holding the great potential for high-efficiency pollutant degradation. When CDs are exposed to light radiation larger than or equal to their optical bandgaps, electrons (e−) leap from the valence band (VB) to the conduction band (CB) while leaving holes (h+) in the VB. Photogenerated electrons could react with the dissolved oxygen to form superoxide radicals (O2 ·‾) while photogenerated holes could react with water to form hydroxyl radicals (·OH). Thereafter, the photogenerated ·OH and O2 ·‾ could in principle interact with the pollutants adsorbed on the photocatalyst surface to mineralize the pollutants. Surface modification of CDs by either chemical doping or formation of semiconductor heterojunction can further enhance their photocatalytic activity [139]. Yue et al. [140] synthesized monoclinic phase bismuth tetroxide microrods/N-doped CDs (m-Bi2O4/NCDs) by a facile hydrothermal treatment. It can be concluded that NCDs boosted the photocatalytic performance of m-Bi2O4 because of the considerable enhancement of the charge transport ability. Meanwhile, Z-type heterojunctions were formed between m-Bi2O4 and NCDs, leading to a higher charge separation efficiency (Fig. 8 a). Taken together, m-Bi2O4/NCDs exhibited an enhanced photocatalytic performance toward methylene orange and phenol.

As previously reported, some CDs exhibited unique up-conversion photoluminescence (UPPL) properties that can successively absorb two or more low-energy photons and then emit high-energy photon [144]. The UPPL property of CDs considerably broadened the light absorption range of CDs-based photocatalysts, therefore achieving efficient use of the full spectrum of solar energy. Wang and coworkers [141] synthesized CDs modified bismuth antimonate (BiSbO4) composite photocatalyst CDs-BiSbO4 (CDBSb) which demonstrated a good degradation performance of ciprofloxacin (CIP). BiSbO4 is known as a typical wide bandgap photocatalyst, and the addition of CDs could broaden the spectral response of the as-prepared composite photocatalyst while maintaining the strong redox ability of BiSbO4. As shown in Fig. 8b, CDs with UPPL properties were synthesized that partially converts wavelength from 550 to 900 nm to wavelength from 320 to 500 nm. Under simulated sunlight conditions, CDBSb achieved two times higher photodegradation performance than that of unmodified BiSbO4.

CDs could act as charge reservoirs to induce charge separation as well as inhibit the recombination of photogenerated charges. Zhang and coworkers [142] synthesized g-C3N4/CDs nanocomposite for phenol degradation. The transient photocurrent response characterization of both as-prepared g-C3N4 and g-C3N4/CDs composites revealed that the g-C3N4/CDs had a higher “on-off” photocurrent response (Fig. 8c). The delayed response of the composite electrodes indicated that CDs captured and stored photoexcited electrons which further promoted the separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs, thus enhancing the photocatalytic efficiency.

CDs with good photoelectron transfer properties could be developed as electron mediator for promoting interfacial charge transfer. EIS characterizations were carried out to investigate photogenerated electron-hole separation processes [145]. Ji et al. [143] synthesized CQDs/BiOBr photocatalysts through a facile hydrothermal method. Fig. 8d compares the Nyquist plots of BiOBr and CQDs/BiOBr, unveiling that CQDs/BiOBr exhibited a small interfacial resistance, verifying that CDs effectively accelerated the interfacial charge transfer process.

Photocatalytic treatment of heavy-metal ions from water mainly involves the reduction of high-valent metal ions compounds to either low-valent ones or even zero-valent metallic particles. In general, photocatalytic treatment of heavy-metal ions requires no additional energy input and facilitates the recovery and reuse of heavy-metal components. It has been revealed that the efficient photocatalytic reduction of Pb2+, Hg2+, Cr6+ and other heavy-metal ions [146–148]. Recently, Wang and coworkers [149] synthesized N23-CQDs/SnS2 photocatalysts for the reduction of Cr(VI) by preparing N23-CQDs and loading them onto SnS2 nanosheets. The addition of N23-CQDs significantly enhances the photocatalytic performance of the nanocomposite. N23-CQDs functioned as an electron acceptor to accelerate electron transfer process and suppressed the recombination of photogenerated hole/electron pairs. The as-prepared samples exhibited an enhanced photocatalytic performance as the loading amount of N23-CQDs was set to 40 %. The photoreduction of Cr6+ by N23-CQDs/SnS2 was achieved through the photogeneration of hole/electron pair under visible-light excitation and photoreduction of toxic and harmful Cr6+ into non-toxic Cr3+ species. In addition, the addition of 4-nitrophenol as a hole sacrificial agent can further accelerate the separation of hole/electron pairs towards a higher photocatalytic efficiency.

Photocatalysis is considered one of the most sustainable method for dye wastewater treatment. CDs and corresponding nanocomposites could be designed for the photodegradation of dye pollutants in water [150]. Wang and coworkers [151] synthesized water-soluble green fluorescent G-CDs by using cornus walteri leaves as carbon source for the photodegradation of methyl orange (MO), MG, and methyl violet (MV). As shown in Fig. 9 a–c, the photodegradation efficiency of MO, MG, and MV by the as-synthesized G-CDs reached 98.0 %, 97.1 % and 63.6 % after 40, 50 and 90 min, respectively. The free radical capture experiments verified that the degradation of the dyes could be accredited to the photogeneration of holes and electrons by G-CDs under the visible-light irradiation. The photogenerated electrons reacted with O2 to form O2 ·‾, the oxidation of water by holes to form ·OH, both of which interacted with the dye molecules to break them down into smaller molecules (Fig. 9d).

TiO2 possesses superior photocatalytic properties as well as low cost [153,154], but exhibiting a wide optical bandgap, low solar energy utilization efficiency, and fast recombination photogenerated hole/electron pairs. Therefore, the surface modification of TiO2 by CDs is considered a promising means to enhance the photocatalytic performance of TiO2. Shen et al. [152] prepared CDs/TNs (carbon dots/TiO2 sheets) nanocomposites that CDs were homogeneously dispersed on the TNs surface, which demonstrated good photodegradation effect on Congo red (CR) and rhodamine B (RhB) (Fig. 9e and f). For the as-fabricated CDs/TNs, the highest photodegradation efficiency of Congo red could reach 85.9 % after 120 min (Fig. 9g and h). In fact, CDs significantly enhanced the photocatalytic performance, which could be ascribed to the fast electron transfer channels form between CDs and TNs for accelerating the photocatalytic reaction (Fig. 9i). Photoelectrochemical measurements further revealed that the use of CDs could effectively accelerate the migration of photogenerated hole and electron (Fig. 9j–l).

As one of energy-saving technologies on water treatment, photocatalysis is widely used for the treatment of numerous intractable pollutants. With respect to the complicated molecular structure of antibiotics, photocatalysis is one of the most promising approaches to degrade antibiotics [155,156]. CDs sensitized photocatalysts could be designed and developed to boost the photocatalytic efficiency. Qu et al. [157] prepared N/S-codoped CDs by employing L-Cys as precursor and further embedded them in ZnO to prepare ZnO/N,S-CQDs nanoflowers. The modification of ZnO nanostructures through the incorporation of N/S-CQDs significantly decreased the optical bandgap and extended the absorption range. The degradation rate of ZnO/N,S-CQDs nanoflowers reached 92.9 % within 20 min for CIP and 86.7 % within 50 min for cephalexin under simulated sunlight irradiation condition, respectively. Under natural sunlight condition, ZnO/N,S-CQDs nanoflowers also exhibited a good photodegradation efficiency for antibiotics. Taking CIP as an example, CIP was degraded by 85.8 % after 50 min under natural sunlight.

Non-metallic photocatalysts are another type of promising candidates for photocatalysis, such as g-C3N4, but its application is majorly limited by the fast electron-hole recombination. Wang and coworkers reported the preparation of hollow tubular g-C3N4 with surface-modified S-doped CQDs (HTCN-C) for the photodegradation of TC, revealing that S-doped CQDs effectively broaden the light absorption range of photocatalyst composite [158]. In comparison with hollow tubular g-C3N4 (HTCN), HTCN-C (2) loaded with 2 mL S-CQDs exhibited a narrower bandgap and shorter lifetime of hole/electron pair. Upon photoexcitation with visible light, the photodegradation of TC by HTCN-C reached 82.67 %, which was considerably higher than that of both HTCN (51.85 %) and bulk g-C3N4 (30.16 %).

The Fenton technique is one of the classical AOPs, which was previously proposed by chemist Fenton in 1984. In particular, tartaric acid can be rapidly oxidized in the presence of both Fe(II) and H2O2 under acidic conditions. Accordingly, the system composed of Fe2+ and H2O2 together is called the Fenton system while Fe2+ and H2O2 are known as Fenton reagents [159]. In fact, plenty of hydroxyl radicals can be generated during the Fenton process, which can effectively degrade organic pollutants in aqueous medium and then mineralize them into CO2 and H2O. For traditional Fenton reagents, it remains unresolved problems that related to accomplish real water treatment. On the one hand, the optimal pH range of Fenton process is quite narrow, typically in the pH range of 2–4, making it necessary to adjust the pH value of wastewater by pre-treatment, which greatly increases the cost. On the other hand, Fenton process is prone to produce excessive iron sludge, which requires subsequent treatment, otherwise it will decrease the efficiency. Moreover, the decomposition of H2O2 and waste of reagents due to Fe2+ instability will influence the performance as well [160–162]. In order to overcome these problems raised by traditional Fenton reagents, researchers have further explored photo-Fenton, electro-Fenton and microwave-Fenton system. Currently, non-homogeneous Fenton oxidation techniques have been developed to avoid the overproduction of iron sludge in the homogeneous system. CDs have been considered a new avenue for wastewater treatment by Fenton method through fast transfer of electron transfer or UPPL properties.

The Fenton reaction generally relies on the generation of ·OH by the reaction of ferrous ions with H2O2 (Eq. (1)). The H2O2 played an indispensable role in triggering a Fenton-like reaction so that the Fe3+ are reduced, again producing ferrous ions with the same oxidizing power of hydrogen peroxide radicals (·HO2) (Eq. (2)). However, the process of reduction of Fe3+ to Fe2+ is quite slow that limiting the practical application of homogeneous Fenton reaction. In fact, Fenton reaction is accompanied by a series of side reactions, which led to the consumption of H2O2 and ·OH and inefficient degradation efficiency. When carbon materials are applied in Fenton and Fenton-like reactions, their strong electron transfer ability can not only accelerate the reduction of Fe3+, but also directly activate hydrogen peroxide to generate reactive oxygen species (ROS) to promote the degradation of pollutants [163].
(1) Fe2++H2O2 → Fe3++·OH + OH−
(2) Fe3++H2O2 → Fe2++·HO2+H+
(3) H2O2+·OH →·HO2+H2O
(4) Fe2++·OH → Fe3++OH−
(5) Fe3++·HO2 → Fe2++O2+ H+
(6) ·OH+·HO2 → H2O2+O2

Phenolic pollutants are a type of organic pollutants with strong toxicity that are difficult to biodegrade. They are widely present in industrial wastewater such as petrochemicals, printing and dyeing, and pesticides. When discharged into natural water bodies, they can cause serious pollution and pose a threat to the ecosystem [164,165]. Fenton reaction and Fenton-like reaction have been explored for the treatment of phenolic pollutants but insufficient efficiency of Fe2+/Fe3+ cycle makes the H2O2 utilization less efficient. Besides, the harsh acidic conditions further limited the practical application of Fenton reaction (Fig. 10 a). With the aim to overcome the disadvantages of the conventional Fenton reaction, Zhang and coworkers [166] synthesized CQDs by electrochemical oxidative stripping method and further constructed CQDs-Fe3+/H2O2 for the treatment of organic pollutants. It was unveiled that Fe3+ was bonded with CQDs through bidentate coordination due to the existence of edge carboxylic groups on CQDs, which further promoted the formation of Fe2+ that achieved by induced electron transfer between CQDs and Fe3+, thereafter exhibiting excellent performance in the degradation of phenol. It should be highlighted that the organic pollutants with electron donor groups could bound to the CQDs with carboxylic groups through hydrogen bonds, forming the intermediate with electron-donor characteristic, which facilitated the generation of ·OH and enhanced the utilization efficiency of H2O2. The electron transfer and deprotonation processes between CQDs, Fe3+ and phenol (CQD-mediated Fe2+/3+ redox) are shown in Fig. 10a. The pseudo-first-order reaction rate constant (K obs) of CQDs-Fe3+/H2O2 was found to be 38-fold higher than that of Fe3+/H2O2. The utilization efficiency of H2O2 (η) of CQDs-Fe3+/H2O2 was 33-fold relative to that of Fe2+/H2O2 at initial half an hour and nearly 19-fold higher than that of Fe3+/H2O2 (Fig. 10b). The toxic intermediates generated during the degradation of phenol by CQDs-Fe3+/H2O2 were significantly reduced. In addition, CQDs with carboxyl groups had a buffering capacity, which widened the pH range of Fenton reaction, showing the unique advantages of CQDs in optimizing Fenton oxidation method.

The introduction of UV or visible light into the Fenton system can be effective in enhancing the degradation efficiency of Fenton method for dye degradation [161,168–170], since the conversion rate of Fe3+ to Fe2+ is enhanced and more hydroxyl radicals were generated. The addition of CQDs is beneficial to further enhance the photo-Fenton catalytic activity of BiFeO3 catalyst [167]. CQD/BiFeO3 can effectively degrade acid orange 7 (AO7) under visible-light and near-infrared light (NIR) illumination (Fig. 10c and d). As seen from Fig. 10e, the reaction of H2O2 with Fe2+ on the BiFeO3 surface under visible-light irradiation produced hydroxyl radicals with Fe3+. CQD/BiFeO3 exhibited photocatalytic and photo-Fenton degradation performance under NIR light irradiation while BiFeO3 could hardly be excited by NIR light irradiation (Fig. 10e and f), which is mainly due to the UPPL characteristic of as-synthesized CQDs (Fig. 10g) that extending the light response region of CQD/BiFeO3 catalyst.

Among numerous antibiotic removal methods, AOPs can convert antibiotics in water into small molecules. Under appropriate conditions, complete mineralization of antibiotics can even be achieved, thereby achieving the treatment of wastewater containing antibiotics [171,172]. As a type of AOPs, the Fenton method, also has such advantages and exhibits excellent degradation effects in the degradation of antibiotics by AOPs [172,173]. The non-homogeneous-phase Fenton catalyst overcame the problems that a large amount of iron sludge generated during the treatment of wastewater and narrow pH range [163]. Tian and coworkers synthesized CQDs and Cu-modified iron oxide with mesoporous characteristic (CQDs/Cu-MIO multiphase Fenton catalysts), respectively [174]. Taking the advantage of the fast transfer characteristic of CQDs, the enhanced electron transfer from Cu2+ to Fe3+ could be realized. The surface modification by CQDs greatly improved the performance for removing ofloxacin under neutral pH conditions, offering a promising approach for ofloxacin contamination.

To achieve the high-efficiency activation of H2O2 in homogeneous Fenton systems, Qian et al. synthesized homogeneous CD-anchored Fe3+ catalysts (CD-COOFe3+) with self-regulated proton-transfer property [175]. It should be highlighted that the use of CD-COOFe3+ activated H2O2 to produce ·OH reaching over two orders of magnitude larger than that of the Fe3+/H2O2 system. The cumulative ·OH concentration in the CD-COOFe3+/H2O2 system was 33 times higher than the Fe3+/H2O2 system due to the formation of •OH through the cleavage of the O–O bond, which was mainly attributed to the fast electron-transfer processes and self-regulated proton-transfer process. The utilization of H2O2 (η) of CD-COOFe3+/H2O2 system for degrading sulfadiazine (SDZ) and sulfamethazine (SMZ) was 71 and 51 times higher than that of Fe3+/H2O2 system. Furthermore, organic pollutants with electron-donating groups faciliated the reduction of Fe3+ through forming hydrogen bonds with carboxyl groups on the edge of CDs. The ability of the CD-COOFe3+/H2O2 system to remove SDZ and SMZ was evaluated, revealing that the removal rates of SDZ and SMZ both exceeded 80 % after 2 h while they could hardly be removed in Fe3+/H2O2 system. In addition, the CD-COOFe3+ catalyst could be recycled through modifying the surface-charge that solving the problem of homogeneous Fenton catalyst recovery.

In this review, recent progress of CDs-based nanomaterials/nanocomposites are systematically summarized toward the practical application in detection and treatment of pollutants in wastewater. Although different synthetic methods and precursors have been developed for enhancing the photocatalytic performance of CDs, further researches are still urgent for the realization of the controllable synthesis, large-scale and low-cost production. From a synthetic chemist perspective, rational design and precise synthesis of CDs with both full-color emission and near-unity PLQY remains a formidable task since the prior achievements are dependent on the time-consuming trial-and-error methods. More recently, it has been reported that machine learning techniques spur the breakthroughs in the design of novel fluorescent materials and LED devices [176–179]. Machine learning could be potentially developed as one of the most prominent and efficient approaches to accelerating the rational design and development of highly-efficient CDs with high PLQYs and controllable emission wavelengths [180–185]. In particular, the data-driven method that combines machine learning with density functional theory calculations will play a vital role in interpreting previous experimental results and further stimulating the optimization the synthetic condition, such as the choice of precursors, catalysts and other experimental conditions.

Most of the current researches have been focused on the detection and treatment of single contaminants under laboratory conditions. However, the real water environment is much more complex which can be influenced by the existence of other substances, pH value, temperature and so on. Considering that CDs with a relatively small size and modular surface-edge groups that is conducive to the combination between CDs and pollutants toward detection and adsorption, the recovery of CDs and CDs-based nanocomposite should be carried out to avoid secondary pollution. Although CDs-based photocatalysts still face some challenges in wastewater treatment, there is no doubt that CDs have great prospects in detecting, adsorbing and degrading various pollutants on the basis PET, ICT, FRET, IFE and other mechanisms. In particular, CDs as one of good pollutant adsorbents could work together with other functional materials to accomplish the high-efficiency removal of water pollutants through hydrophobic interaction, electrostatic force, π-conjugated interaction, hydrogen bonding, and surface complexation, respectively. The optical properties and electron transfer ability of CDs make them as an idea choice for the design of composite-based photocatalysts that CDs could act as photocatalysts or co-catalysts to realize the photocatalytic treatment of wastewater pollutants or enhance the photocatalytic performance. Moreover, CDs could also be utilized in the Fenton and Fenton-like processes for the wastewater treatment to promote the cycling of Fe3+ and Fe2+ toward more efficient degradation of pollutants.

Compared to other functional nanomaterials, CDs hold a great promise in the application of sensitive detection and efficient treatment of water pollutants in the aspect of low-cost and rapid synthesis, full-color emission color with high PLQY and environmentally-friendly characteristics. Toward sustainable development of wastewater treatment technology, machine learning technology could further stimulate the rational design and synthesis of CDs through the choice of low-cost precursors and renewable biomass.

Hui Li: Writing – original draft. Xicheng Li: Writing – original draft. Shuyan Wei: Writing – original draft. Changzheng Wang: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Conceptualization. Yang Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.
